course_subtitle: A comprehensive course on the OCP NIC 3.0 Design Specification

modules.title: Introduction to OCP NIC 3.0
modules.subtitle: Overview, tenets, form factors, capabilities, and the multi-vendor ecosystem.

slides.title: OCP NIC 3.0 Technical Overview
slides.subtitle: A comprehensive course on the OCP NIC 3.0 Design Specification

slides.title: Six Modules, One Complete Picture
slides.subtitle: From form factors to compliance - everything you need to design, integrate, and deploy OCP NIC 3.0 solutions.

bullets.text: NIC specification purpose-built for hyperscale data centers.
Defines mechanical, electrical, thermal, and management requirements in a single document.
Field-replaceable design enabling a multi-vendor ecosystem.
Not backward compatible because of the new connector and new mechanical form factor.
Created by the OCP NIC Subproject under the OCP Server Project.

slides.title: Open Compute Tenets
slides.subtitle: Five principles that shape every design decision in the OCP NIC 3.0 specification.

cards.title: Openness
cards.body: Open specification, IP-free design, and unified form factors accessible to the entire industry.

cards.title: Efficiency
cards.body: Size-optimized footprint, thermally efficient design, and power-optimized operation at scale.

cards.title: Impact
cards.body: Multi-vendor removable NICs that create a robust, competitive supply chain ecosystem.

cards.title: Scalability
cards.body: Common remote management interfaces and architecture designed for next-gen compatibility.

cards.title: Sustainability
cards.body: Community-authored standards, cost-focused design, and common materials for longevity.

slides.title: Five Form Factors, One Ecosystem
slides.subtitle: Scalable options from single-width to double-width - each tailored to different deployment needs.
rows: Not recommended for new mainline designs

slides.title: Key Capabilities
slides.subtitle: A rich feature set designed for modern data center workloads and operational requirements.

cards.title: PCIe Gen 6 Interface
cards.body: 64 GT/s signaling rate with up to 32 lanes in DSFF configurations for maximum throughput.

cards.title: Multi-Host & Multi-Root
cards.body: Supports single-root, multi-root, and multi-host topologies for flexible system architectures.

cards.title: SmartNIC / DPU Ready
cards.body: On-board DRAM and accelerator support for SmartNIC and DPU implementations.

cards.title: Hot-Swap FRU
cards.body: Field-replaceable unit design enables live insertion and removal without system downtime.

cards.title: NC-SI Sideband Management
cards.body: Standardized out-of-band management channel for BMC communication and network control.

cards.title: FRU EEPROM
cards.body: On-card identification EEPROM for asset tracking, card identification, and inventory management.

cards.title: Thermal Test Fixtures
cards.body: Standardized thermal test fixtures ensure consistent validation across vendors and implementations.

slides.title: Non-NIC Use Cases
slides.subtitle: The OCP NIC 3.0 form factor isn't just for NICs - it's a platform for diverse I/O devices.

cards.title: PCIe Retimer
cards.body: Signal conditioning and retiming for extended PCIe reach in large-scale systems.

cards.title: Accelerator
cards.body: Compute accelerators and co-processors leveraging the standardized hot-swap slot.

cards.title: NVMe Storage
cards.body: High-performance NVMe storage controllers in a hot-swappable, standardized form factor.

cards.title: Storage HBA
cards.body: Host bus adapters for SAS, SATA, or hybrid storage connectivity.

cards.title: Timing / Sync
cards.body: Precision timing cards for PTP and network synchronization applications.

cards.title: Custom I/O
cards.body: Any PCIe device that fits the electrical and mechanical envelope of the specification.

slides.title: Contributing Companies
slides.subtitle: 16 industry leaders collaborating to define the open NIC standard for the data center.

bullets.text: Connector, silicon, system, hyperscale, and test organizations all contributed to the v1.6 specification.
That mix is what turns a card form factor into a practical multi-vendor ecosystem.
The acknowledged companies span the supply chain needed to design, build, validate, and deploy OCP NIC 3.0 platforms.

figure.alt: Real logo assets for the 16 companies acknowledged as contributors to the OCP NIC 3.0 version 1.6 specification.
figure.caption: Contributor logos for companies acknowledged by the OCP NIC 3.0 v1.6 specification.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
items: OCP NIC 3.0 is an open, IP-free specification that standardizes NIC design for hyperscale data centers, enabling true multi-vendor interoperability.
The spec covers mechanical, electrical, thermal, and management requirements - a complete design blueprint in a single document.
Four active form factors (SFF, TSFF, DSFF, TDSFF) scale from 16 to 32 PCIe Gen 6 lanes and 80 W to 160 W power envelopes.
The platform extends beyond NICs to support accelerators, storage, retimers, and timing cards using the same slot infrastructure.
16 major companies collaborate on the specification, driving a healthy, competitive multi-vendor ecosystem.
OCP NIC 3.0 succeeds Mezz 2.0 but is not backward compatible - it introduces a new connector, form factor, and Gen 6 interface.

slides.title: Check Your Platform Mental Model
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and retries are available.

questions.prompt: What is the core interoperability goal of OCP NIC 3.0?

choices.text: Any compliant card should work in any compliant baseboard slot.
Every NIC must use the same silicon vendor.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The standard is multi-vendor. It standardizes the slot and card interfaces, not the silicon supplier.

choices.text: OCP Mezz 2.0 cards should remain plug-compatible.
choices.feedback_incorrect: OCP NIC 3.0 is a follow-on to Mezz 2.0, but it is not backward compatible.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The common mechanical, electrical, thermal, and management requirements enable slot/card interoperability.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the definition slide: the goal is compliant-card and compliant-slot interoperability across vendors.

questions.prompt: Which device types can reasonably use the OCP NIC 3.0 slot infrastructure when they fit the envelope?

choices.text: Network interface cards
PCIe retimer cards
NVMe storage controllers
Devices that ignore the mechanical and electrical envelope

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The slot can support NIC and non-NIC I/O devices when they respect the shared constraints.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Keep the envelope in mind: non-NIC use cases are possible because they reuse the same mechanical and electrical slot constraints.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 1: Introduction to OCP NIC 3.0.

modules.title: Form Factors & Mechanical Design
modules.subtitle: Dimensions, connectors, DSFF slot conversion, faceplates, retention mechanisms, and card assembly.

slides.title: Form Factors & Mechanical Design
slides.subtitle: Dimensions, connectors, faceplates, retention mechanisms, and card assembly.

slides.title: What You'll Learn
objectives: Identify the key dimensions of each OCP NIC 3.0 form factor
Explain the differences between SFF, TSFF, DSFF, and TDSFF
Describe the SFF-TA-1002 4C+ gold finger connector and its mating sequence
Understand faceplate configurations and card retention mechanisms
Explain how DSFF cards relate to SFF slots on the baseboard

slides.title: Form Factor Dimensions
slides.subtitle: Table 5 dimensions, with baseboard pitch nuance from Section 3.1.2.

slides.title: SFF & TSFF Share One Electrical Model
slides.subtitle: The tall variant changes the available Z-height, not the connector model.

cards.title: 2x QSFP Ports
cards.body: High-speed optical connectivity for 100/200/400 GbE deployments using QSFP56 or QSFP-DD modules.

cards.title: 4x SFP Ports
cards.body: Breakout configuration for 10/25 GbE per-port connectivity using standard SFP28 or SFP56 transceivers.

cards.title: 4x RJ45 Ports
cards.body: Copper connectivity for 1/2.5/5/10 GBase-T deployments requiring direct copper cabling.

bullets.text: SFF and TSFF use one 4C+ Primary Connector.
Both support up to 16 PCIe lanes and the same electrical parameters.
Both support up to 80 W delivered power to the card edge.
TSFF keeps the SFF footprint but uses a taller faceplate for larger optical cages, heatsinks, or components.

figure.alt: Diagram comparing SFF and TSFF cards with the same 4C+ connector, up to 16 PCIe lanes, and different faceplate heights.
figure.caption: SFF and TSFF share the same connector and electrical model; TSFF adds vertical space for taller components.

slides.title: DSFF & TDSFF Convert Two SFF Positions
slides.subtitle: Dual-width cards reuse adjacent SFF slot infrastructure with a different mechanical integration model.

bullets.text: DSFF uses Primary and Secondary 4C+ connector positions.
The dual-width family supports up to 32 PCIe lanes.
The maximum delivered card-edge power is 160 W across both connectors.
A DSFF slot is made from two adjacent SFF positions after the middle guide rail is removed.
The DSFF connector pitch is 81.55 mm, so not every pair of normal SFF faceplates maps cleanly back to DSFF.

figure.alt: Diagram showing two adjacent SFF slots converting to one DSFF slot after guide rail removal.
figure.caption: DSFF is not just a wider faceplate; it depends on the connector pitch, guide-rail, and dual-connector slot model.

cards.title: SFF-TA-1002 Compliant
cards.body: Industry-standard edge connector specification ensuring multi-vendor interoperability across all OCP NIC 3.0 implementations.

cards.title: Two-Stage Mating
cards.body: Power pins mate first, then signal pins engage - enabling safe hot-swap insertion and removal without system disruption.

cards.title: Full 16-Lane PCIe
cards.body: Supports full 16-lane PCIe Gen 6 interface; downshifting to 2C+ connector for 8 lanes or fewer applications.

cards.title: DSFF Connector Rules
cards.body: Primary connector must be full 4C+; secondary may downshift to 2C+. Both connectors must be present for mechanical retention.

cards.title: Mechanical Retention
cards.body: Both DSFF connectors must be physically present regardless of lane count to ensure proper card seating and retention in the slot.

figure.alt: Gold finger requirements page from the OCP NIC 3.0 specification
figure.caption: Spec page 40 anchors the SFF-TA-1002 gold-finger requirement.

cards.title: Pull Tab
cards.body: Low-profile, compact retention mechanism ideal for high-density deployments where minimal clearance is available.

cards.title: Ejector Latch
cards.body: Lever mechanism providing greater mechanical advantage for card extraction, well-suited for DSFF cards.

cards.title: Internal Lock
cards.body: Prevents accidental card removal caused by vibration or handling during transport and operation.

slides.title: Module 2 Summary
items: SFF/TSFF are single-width, up to 16 lanes and 80 W; DSFF/TDSFF are dual-width, up to 32 lanes and 160 W.
SFF-TA-1002 4C+ uses two-stage mating: power first, then signals.
Tall variants add 2.7 mm of faceplate/topside height for larger optical modules and heatsinks.
DSFF support is a baseboard slot-layout decision; card-side insulator/backing plate is part of the card.
Retention mechanisms include pull tab, ejector latch, and internal lock.
2C+ downshifting supports lower lane-count applications while preserving key management paths.

slides.title: Check Your Mechanical Fit
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and retries are available.

questions.prompt: Which pairing correctly describes the single-width and dual-width form-factor families?

choices.text: SFF/TSFF are single-width; DSFF/TDSFF are dual-width.
SFF/DSFF are single-width; TSFF/TDSFF are tall-only variants with no lane impact.
choices.feedback_incorrect: DSFF is dual-width. The T prefix indicates a tall variant within the single- or dual-width family.

choices.text: All four active form factors use one connector and 16 lanes.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Dual-width DSFF/TDSFF use two connector positions and can support up to 32 PCIe lanes.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The SFF family is single-width, and the DSFF family spans two adjacent slot positions.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Separate width from height: SFF versus DSFF is width; TSFF and TDSFF are tall variants.

questions.prompt: Which statements are accurate for mechanical integration?

choices.text: The card-side insulator or backing plate is part of the card-side design.
An 89 mm SFF pitch supports all three faceplate variants in the same platform.
DSFF mechanical retention requires both connector locations to be present.
A DSFF installation requires adding an insulator between an already-built card and the baseboard.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Those details keep the mechanical story aligned with the spec and reviewer corrections.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Distinguish baseboard slot conversion from card-side construction. The insulator/backing plate belongs to the card design.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 2: Form Factors & Mechanical Design.

modules.title: Electrical Interface
modules.subtitle: Connector signal groups, slot ownership, DSFF discovery, bifurcation, power states, and hot-swap scope.

slides.title: Electrical Interface
slides.subtitle: Connector pinouts, bifurcation, power states, and hot-swap design.

slides.title: Signals You Will Map
slides.subtitle: What you will be able to do after completing this module.
objectives: 1 Describe the 4C+ connector signal groups and the OCP Bay extension
2 Compare SFF and DSFF connector usage, lane ranges, and management-path assumptions
3 Map baseboard-owned, card-owned, and shared signals across the slot boundary
4 Explain PCIe bifurcation and how DSFF link modes are discovered
5 Walk through mandatory power states, rail sequencing, and hot-swap scope

slides.title: 4C+ Connector Architecture
slides.subtitle: Think in signal groups first; pin tables are easier once the groups are clear.

bullets.text: PCIe lanes, REFCLK, and PERST# form the host-link foundation.
PRSNTB# and BIF# pins communicate card presence and lane configuration.
+3.3V_EDGE, +12V_EDGE, AUX_PWR_EN, MAIN_PWR_EN, and NIC_PWR_GOOD coordinate state transitions.
SMBus, USB, WAKE#, NC-SI over RBT, slot ID, and scan-chain pins support management, wake, and service workflows.

figure.alt: Diagram grouping 4C+ connector signals into PCIe, reset, clock, present, bifurcation, power, management, wake, RBT, and scan-chain groups.
figure.caption: The 4C+ interface combines PCIe, power, management, and OCP Bay sideband signals in one connector family.

slides.title: SFF vs DSFF Pinout
slides.subtitle: DSFF extends the SFF slot concept, but it does not become two independent management channels.

bullets.text: One Primary 4C+ connector carries lanes 0-15, REFCLK/PERST 0-3, and the main management path.
Primary and Secondary connector positions extend the lane map up to lanes 0-31.
A single power domain spans both DSFF connectors.
FRU EEPROM stays on the Primary connector path; secondary manageability pins are not a redundant independent DSFF channel.

figure.alt: Diagram comparing SFF one-connector lanes 0-15 with DSFF primary and secondary connector lanes 0-31.
figure.caption: SFF is a one-connector electrical model; DSFF extends lane capacity through a second connector while keeping one card-level power and management model.

slides.title: Who Owns Which Slot Signals?
slides.subtitle: The interface works because ownership is explicit across the baseboard/card boundary.

bullets.text: The platform supplies rails, drives REFCLK, PERST#, BIF#, AUX_PWR_EN, and MAIN_PWR_EN, and reads PRSNTB and FRU data.
The card straps PRSNTB to PRSNTA, exposes FRU EEPROM records, latches BIF values, and asserts NIC_PWR_GOOD when rails are valid.
SMBus, MCTP bindings, NC-SI over RBT, RBT_ISOLATE, USB, and WAKE# depend on the implemented management path.
The Secondary connector extends lanes, REFCLK, and PERST signals, while core identity and management remain anchored on the Primary path.

figure.alt: Diagram separating baseboard-owned, card-owned, and shared OCP NIC 3.0 slot signals.
figure.caption: Signal ownership is the practical integration checklist: drive, read, latch, or expose each signal from the correct side of the slot.

bullets.text: PRSNTB# and BIF[2:0]# deterministically set the lane width for the supported card/baseboard combination.
BIF[5:0]# spans Primary and Secondary connector behavior.
A DSFF baseboard reads FRU OEM records 0xD0, 0xD1, and 0xD2 in ID Mode to determine supported link subdivision.
BIF pins are driven with the rising edge of AUX_PWR_EN and latched once AUX_PWR_EN and NIC_PWR_GOOD are asserted.

figure.alt: PCIe bifurcation decoder page from the OCP NIC 3.0 specification
figure.caption: Spec page 156 explains bifurcation encoding and DSFF OEM-record discovery.

bullets.text: Four mandatory normal states: NIC Power Off, ID Mode, Aux Power Mode, and Main Power Mode.
Programming Mode is optional for field update behavior, not a normal post-Main step.
+3.3V_EDGE is active in ID, Aux, and Main Power modes.
+12V_EDGE is on in Main Power and may be optional in ID or Programming modes.
PERST# is asserted low in Aux Power; NIC_PWR_GOOD first asserts when AUX-domain rails are within tolerance.

figure.alt: Silent animation showing the mandatory OCP NIC 3.0 power sequence from NIC Power Off through ID, Aux, and Main Power modes, with Programming Mode as an optional branch.
figure.caption: The mandatory path is Power Off to ID to Aux to Main; Programming Mode remains an optional field-update branch.

cards.title: +3.3V_EDGE
cards.body: ±9% tolerance; active in ID, Aux, and Main Power modes.

cards.title: +12V_EDGE
cards.body: +8% / -12% tolerance; powers main ASIC, PHY, and high-speed circuitry.

cards.title: Slot Power Envelopes
cards.body: 15 W to 160 W; SFF up to 80 W, DSFF up to 160 W.

cards.title: FRU EEPROM Declaration
cards.body: Card declares power draw so the baseboard can enforce slot limits.

cards.title: Hot Swap Scope
cards.body: Card provides presence and control signals; platform-side controller design handles the dedicated hot-swap implementation.

slides.title: Module 3 Summary
items: 4C+ carries PCIe data, reference clocks, resets, power, management, wake, and OCP Bay sideband signals.
SFF uses one Primary connector; DSFF extends lane capacity through Primary and Secondary connector positions.
Baseboard, card, and shared-signal ownership determines who drives, reads, latches, or exposes each interface.
DSFF bifurcation depends on PRSNTB encoding and FRU OEM records, not only a static SFF-style table.
Normal power behavior has four mandatory states; Programming Mode is optional field-update behavior.
Hot-swap implementation is primarily platform-side, while the card exposes the required presence and control signals.

slides.title: Check Your Electrical Sequence
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and retries are available.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes signal ownership across the OCP NIC 3.0 slot?

choices.text: The baseboard drives clocks, resets, BIF pins, and power enables; the card exposes identity and asserts NIC_PWR_GOOD when its rails are valid.
The card independently drives all platform clocks and resets after insertion.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The platform drives REFCLK, PERST#, and power-enable behavior. The card responds with identity, latching, and power-good behavior.

choices.text: DSFF secondary management pins form a fully redundant independent management channel.
choices.feedback_incorrect: For DSFF, core identity and management remain anchored on the Primary connector path; the secondary connector extends the slot behavior.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The slot interface depends on clear baseboard and card responsibilities.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the signal ownership slide. The key is who drives, who reads, who latches, and who exposes each signal.

questions.prompt: Which statements are accurate for electrical integration?

choices.text: SFF uses BIF[2:0]#; DSFF uses BIF[5:0]#.
DSFF link modes can be discovered from FRU OEM records.
+3.3V_EDGE is active in ID, Aux, and Main Power modes.
Hot-swap controller implementation is required on both card and baseboard by the spec.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. You kept bifurcation, discovery, rail state, and hot-swap scope separate.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Separate card signals from platform implementation, and remember that DSFF discovery uses FRU OEM records in addition to pins.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 3: Electrical Interface.

motion_intro.subtitle: Connector signal groups, slot ownership, DSFF discovery, bifurcation, power states, and hot-swap scope.

modules.title: Management & Firmware
modules.subtitle: NC-SI sideband, FRU EEPROM, temperature monitoring, and firmware updates.

slides.title: Management & Firmware
slides.subtitle: NC-SI sideband, FRU EEPROM, temperature monitoring, and firmware updates.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to:
objectives: 1 Describe the three management interface types and explain why RBT + MCTP is the preferred combination
2 Explain NC-SI sideband communication including control traffic and pass-through traffic between BMC and NIC
3 Understand FRU EEPROM structure including IPMI format and OEM MultiRecords (0xC0, 0xD0-D3)
4 Identify temperature monitoring, power reporting, and firmware update requirements for compliant cards

slides.title: Management Interface Types
slides.subtitle: Three management types are defined - every compliant card must support at least one.

cards.title: RBT + MCTP (preferred)
cards.body: NC-SI over RBT for real-time control/pass-through plus MCTP for platform management.

cards.title: RBT Type
cards.body: NC-SI over RMII physical interface, separate from host PCIe.

cards.title: MCTP Type
cards.body: Platform management over bindings such as SMBus/I2C, PCIe VDM, or USB.

bullets.text: NC-SI carries BMC control traffic and network pass-through traffic.
Operates over the RBT sideband path, independent of the host CPU.
Token-based arbitration prevents multi-slot bus contention.
RBT_ISOLATE prevents a powered-down slot from pulling the shared bus low.
Complete RBT hot-swap behavior is outside the specification scope.

figure.alt: NC-SI over RBT topology page from the OCP NIC 3.0 specification
figure.caption: Spec page 126 provides RBT topology context for sideband management and arbitration.

slides.title: FRU EEPROM
slides.subtitle: Every OCP NIC 3.0 card carries a mandatory identification EEPROM for asset tracking and capability declaration.

cards.title: 0xC0 Base OEM
cards.body: Form factor, management type, PCIe lanes, power, and cooling tier declarations.

cards.title: 0xD0 / 0xD1 DSFF Link Subdivision
cards.body: Defines how DSFF PCIe links map across Primary and Secondary connectors.

cards.title: 0xD2 Extended OEM
cards.body: Additional capability flags and extended configuration parameters.

cards.title: 0xD3 Flex I/O
cards.body: Flexible I/O configuration declarations for custom interface mappings.

figure.alt: FRU OEM record page from the OCP NIC 3.0 specification
figure.caption: Spec page 201 anchors the Base OEM record and related FRU content.

slides.title: Temperature, Power & Firmware
slides.subtitle: Operational monitoring and update mechanisms that keep OCP NIC 3.0 cards healthy and current.

cards.title: Temperature
cards.body: Required for cards with ≥5 W TDP. Accuracy ±3°C. Three thresholds defined:

cards.title: Power
cards.body: Power requirements are declared in the FRU EEPROM OEM records. The baseboard reads these values and verifies the slot can supply the required power before transitioning to Main Power state.

cards.title: Firmware
cards.body: Must support UEFI FMP (Firmware Management Protocol) for standardized updates. NIST 800-193 recommended for authenticated images, rollback protection, and secure boot chain.

slides.title: Module 4 Summary
items: RBT + MCTP is preferred because it combines NC-SI sideband control/pass-through with platform management.
MCTP can bind over SMBus/I2C, PCIe VDM, or USB depending on implementation.
NC-SI enables BMC-to-NIC control and pass-through traffic independent of the host CPU.
The arbitration ring prevents bus contention; RBT_ISOLATE prevents a powered-down slot from pulling the bus low.
FRU EEPROM is mandatory and uses IPMI format with OEM records 0xC0 and 0xD0-0xD3.
Temperature reporting, power declaration, and UEFI FMP firmware update support round out management readiness.

slides.title: Check Your Management Path
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and retries are available.

questions.prompt: Why is RBT + MCTP the preferred management combination?

choices.text: It combines NC-SI real-time control/pass-through with MCTP platform management.
It removes the need for FRU EEPROM content.
choices.feedback_incorrect: FRU EEPROM remains mandatory. RBT + MCTP describes management transport behavior.

choices.text: It makes host PCIe the only management path.
choices.feedback_incorrect: RBT is a sideband physical path, and MCTP can use platform management bindings.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The preferred option combines NC-SI behavior and richer MCTP management capabilities.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the management-types slide. The preferred option combines complementary management paths.

questions.prompt: Which statements are accurate for management and identity?

choices.text: NC-SI can support BMC control and pass-through traffic.
RBT_ISOLATE prevents a powered-down slot from pulling the bus low.
FRU EEPROM includes OEM records such as 0xC0 and 0xD0 through 0xD3.
Hot-swap over RBT is fully specified as an in-scope feature.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Those are the key management details this module needs learners to retain.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Keep the scope clean: RBT isolation and arbitration are in scope; a complete RBT hot-swap design is not.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 4: Management & Firmware.

modules.title: Signal Integrity, Thermal & Environmental
modules.subtitle: PCIe channel budgets, airflow, cooling tiers, and shock/vibration testing.

slides.title: Signal Integrity, Thermal & Environmental
slides.subtitle: PCIe channel budgets, airflow, cooling tiers, and shock/vibration testing.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to:
objectives: 1 Explain PCIe signal integrity budget split between baseboard and NIC
2 Describe two airflow orientations and their impact on thermal design
3 Apply thermal design guidelines and reference heatsink dimensions
4 Identify cooling tiers and thermal test fixture methodology
5 Describe shock and vibration testing requirements

bullets.text: PCIe Gen 6 operation depends on shared baseboard and card channel budgets.
Baseboard supplies the 100 MHz differential reference clock.
NIC receive pins use AC coupling capacitors.
CLB validates the baseboard side; CBB validates the NIC card side.

figure.alt: Compliance Load Board page from the OCP NIC 3.0 specification
figure.caption: Spec pages 225-226 define fixture-based validation for the two sides of the PCIe channel.
Recovered figure group: Hot Aisle Airflow Direction; Cold Aisle Airflow Direction
Recovered figure group: ASIC Supportable Power - Hot Aisle; ASIC Supportable Power - Cold Aisle

bullets.text: 12 tiers defined in the specification
Tiers 1-6: typical server deployments
Tiers 7+: higher power, enhanced heatsinks/airflow needed
Cooling tier declared in FRU EEPROM
Sheet metal enclosure replicating server airflow
3 candlestick sensors at card inlet
Wind tunnel attachment for calibrated airflow
CFD models and fixture designs on OCP wiki/GitHub
Mandatory for all OCP NIC 3.0 cards
Vertical shock table with controlled impulses
SFF fixture: tests 4 cards simultaneously
DSFF fixture: tests 4 cards (scaled dimensions)
Dye and pull test methodology
Tests plating defects, adhesion, and wear patterns
Validates connector reliability over insertion cycles

figure.alt: SFF and DSFF Shock and Vibe Fixtures
figure.caption: SFF and DSFF Shock and Vibe Fixtures

slides.title: Key Takeaways
items: PCIe signal integrity is a shared budget - baseboard supplies 100 MHz REFCLK, NIC has AC coupling caps.
CLB and CBB compliance fixtures enable independent validation of baseboard and NIC card sides.
Cards must work in both hot aisle (55°C, card edge) and cold aisle (25-35°C, faceplate) orientations.
Reference SFF heatsink: ~12 W hot aisle, ~19 W cold aisle at 150 LFM - cold aisle provides ~60% more headroom.
12 cooling tiers (1-6 typical), declared in FRU EEPROM for system-level thermal management.
Shock/vibration testing is mandatory; gold finger durability verified via dye and pull test.

slides.title: Check Your Reliability Model
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and retries are available.

questions.prompt: What is the key signal-integrity responsibility split?

choices.text: The baseboard and NIC each meet their portion of the PCIe channel budget.
Only the NIC card is responsible for the full Gen 6 channel.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The channel is shared. Both baseboard and card budgets matter.

choices.text: The connector eliminates the need for compliance fixtures.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The spec defines CLB and CBB fixtures so each side can be validated independently.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Reliable Gen 6 operation depends on both sides meeting their allocations.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the signal-integrity slide: it is a shared budget with independent validation fixtures.

questions.prompt: Which items belong in thermal and environmental validation?

choices.text: Hot-aisle and cold-aisle airflow orientation
Cooling tier declaration in FRU EEPROM
Shock/vibration and gold-finger durability tests
Assuming bottom-side airflow cools the card

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Thermal and environmental validation spans airflow, tiering, fixture methodology, and durability testing.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Remember the airflow constraint: cooling is handled from the top-side heatsink path, not bottom-side airflow.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 5: Signal Integrity, Thermal & Environmental.

modules.title: Compliance, Ecosystem & Future
modules.subtitle: Regulatory standards, v1.6.0 implementation changes, ecosystem resources, and the roadmap ahead.

slides.title: Compliance, Ecosystem & Future
slides.subtitle: Regulatory standards, v1.6.0 changes, ecosystem, and the roadmap ahead.

slides.title: What You Will Learn
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to:
objectives: 1 Identify required regulatory and safety compliance areas for OCP NIC 3.0 cards
2 Explain the major version 1.6.0 changes that affect implementation planning
3 Use the OCP NIC Subproject, specification, and marketplace resources after the course
4 Distinguish active SFF/DSFF design paths from deprecated LFF historical content

slides.title: Regulatory Compliance
slides.subtitle: The card has required environmental, EMC, safety, and ESD obligations, while some emissions and immunity work is completed at system level.

bullets.text: China RoHS, EU RoHS 2, REACH, and related material restrictions apply to OCP NIC 3.0 cards.
Class A emissions and regional requirements include FCC, EN 55032, CE/EMC Directive, VCCI, KN32/KN35, and BSMI targets.
Product safety and electrostatic-discharge immunity are part of the required compliance set.
Emissions and immunity tests are completed at system level, so NIC vendors coordinate with system vendors.
Recommended compliance includes WEEE reporting and additional EMC margin practices such as CISPR 32 or VCCI-style requirements.

slides.title: What Changed in v1.6.0?
slides.subtitle: The latest release tightened implementation details, not just wording.

cards.title: DSFF Matured
cards.body: Complete mechanical, electrical, and pinout definitions. Production-ready for deployment.

cards.title: PCIe Link Subdivision
cards.body: New OEM records 0xD0/0xD1 declare lane division between DSFF connectors.

cards.title: Extended OEM Record (0xD2)
cards.body: Additional capability flags beyond the base OEM record for richer NIC descriptions.

cards.title: Flex I/O Record (0xD3)
cards.body: Framework for flexible I/O configuration declarations in FRU EEPROM.

cards.title: LFF Deprecated
cards.body: Large Form Factor removed from the spec. Use SFF or DSFF instead. Moved to appendix.

cards.title: Cooling Tiers Defined
cards.body: 12 tiers fully specified. SFF thermal complete. TSFF/DSFF/TDSFF thermal TBD.

bullets.text: PCIe Gen 6 / 64 GT/s guidance appears with OCP-specific fixture and insertion-loss caveats.
PRSNTB encoding plus FRU OEM records 0xD0, 0xD1, and 0xD2 describe DSFF link subdivision.
Aux Mode uses WAKE# or NC-SI over RBT; Wake is not applicable in Main Power Mode.
Extended OEM and Flex I/O records moved to 0xD2 and 0xD3 to avoid MHS M-PNP conflicts.
LFF content moved to Appendix 9 for historical context; SFF and DSFF are the active design paths.

figure.alt: Diagram summarizing OCP NIC 3.0 v1.6 changes for Gen 6 context, DSFF discovery, power states, FRU records, and LFF deprecation.
figure.caption: Version 1.6.0 is most useful as an implementation checklist: signal integrity, DSFF discovery, power-state semantics, FRU records, and form-factor direction.

slides.title: Ecosystem & Adoption
slides.subtitle: A broad and growing ecosystem of NIC vendors, server platforms, and compliance testing supports the OCP NIC 3.0 standard.

bullets.text: Multiple major silicon vendors ship OCP NIC 3.0 compliant NICs and DPUs
Products span standard NICs, SmartNICs, and DPUs across SFF and DSFF form factors
Multi-vendor interoperability ensures a competitive supply chain
Independent compliance testing available through accredited interoperability labs
Open test fixtures — thermal, shock/vibration, and PCIe compliance boards available to all vendors
Vendor-neutral validation gives customers confidence in cross-vendor interoperability
▶ TSFF/DSFF/TDSFF thermal characterization and cooling tier completion
▶ 1.6T Ethernet - next-generation network bandwidth targets
▶ PCIe Gen 7 - next-generation interface signaling rate
▶ Liquid cooling TSFF - liquid cooling solutions for single-width form factor
▶ Enhanced DPU form factors with expanded power envelopes for accelerated workloads
▶ Liquid cooling TDSFF - liquid cooling solutions for dual-width tall form factor

cards.title: OCP Wiki
cards.body: Central repository for specs, meeting minutes, design files, test fixtures, and compliance boards.

cards.title: OCP Marketplace
cards.body: Directory of commercially available OCP-conformant products from certified vendors.

cards.title: OCP NIC GitHub
cards.body: TTF MCU software and other open-source resources for the OCP NIC 3.0 ecosystem.

cards.title: Related Specs
cards.body: DC-MHS, DC-SCM, and SFF-TA-1002 companion specifications for the full platform picture.

cards.title: OCP NIC Subproject
cards.body: Regular meetings open to all OCP member organizations. Join the conversation.

cards.title: OCP Global Summit
cards.body: Annual events with vendor showcases, deep-dives, and community collaboration.

bullets.text: 1 Introduction - Open, multi-vendor NIC standard with 4 form factors and 16 contributing companies driving the ecosystem.
2 Mechanical - SFF/TSFF/DSFF/TDSFF dimensions, 4C+ connector rules, guide-rail conversion, and retention mechanisms.
3 Electrical - PCIe Gen 6 context, signal ownership, bifurcation discovery, power states, and 15-160 W slot envelopes.
4 Management - RBT, MCTP, NC-SI sideband behavior, FRU EEPROM identity, and DSFF OEM records.
5 Signal & Thermal - Signal integrity budgets, hot/cold aisle thermal design, and cooling tiers for airflow planning.
6 Compliance & Future - Regulatory compliance, v1.6.0 change awareness, OCP resources, and active SFF/DSFF design direction.

slides.title: Check Your Launch Readiness
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and retries are available.

questions.prompt: Which v1.6.0 change most directly affects DSFF electrical integration planning?

choices.text: DSFF link subdivision discovery through PRSNTB encoding and FRU OEM records.
Replacing all SFF slots with LFF slots.
choices.feedback_incorrect: LFF is deprecated and retained in Appendix 9 for historical context, not promoted as the active design path.

choices.text: Removing the need to read card FRU data.
choices.feedback_incorrect: FRU data becomes more important for DSFF capability discovery, not less important.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. DSFF integration depends on both pin encoding and FRU OEM record discovery.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the v1.6.0 slide: DSFF discovery, FRU records, power-state clarifications, Gen 6 context, and LFF deprecation are the big planning points.

questions.prompt: Which resources help continue OCP NIC 3.0 work after this course?

choices.text: The OCP NIC Subproject page and regular calls
The OCP Marketplace NIC listings
The v1.6 specification and related platform specs
Ignoring the subproject and treating the course as the only source

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The course is an entry point; the spec, subproject, and marketplace keep the learner connected.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Use the course as a guide, then go to the living OCP resources for implementation details and community work.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 6: Compliance, Ecosystem & Future.
slides.cert_message: You've completed the OCP Academy NIC course and can use the v1.6 specification, NIC Subproject resources, and marketplace references for deeper implementation work.

motion_intro.subtitle: Regulatory standards, v1.6.0 implementation changes, ecosystem resources, and the roadmap ahead.

term_glossary.tooltip: Open Compute Project network interface card specification for interoperable, removable server NICs.
Small Form Factor OCP NIC 3.0 card with one 4C+ connector.
Tall Small Form Factor variant with the SFF board footprint and taller faceplate/topside Z-height.
Dual Small Form Factor card using two adjacent connector positions for up to 32 PCIe lanes.
Tall Dual Small Form Factor variant of DSFF with taller faceplate/topside Z-height.
SFF-TA-1002 card-edge connector used by OCP NIC 3.0, including 4C and OCP Bay regions.
Hardware configuration that splits PCIe lanes into one or more links through BIF and presence pins.
Network Controller Sideband Interface for BMC-to-NIC control and network pass-through traffic.
Management Component Transport Protocol used for platform management over bindings such as SMBus/I2C, PCIe VDM, or USB.
Field-replaceable unit EEPROM storing identity, capability, power, cooling, and OEM record data.
Reduced Media Independent Interface Based Transport used as an NC-SI sideband physical interface.
Compliance Load Board used to validate the baseboard side of the PCIe channel.
Compliance Breakout Board used to validate the NIC card side of the PCIe channel.
FRU-declared thermal capability tier used to communicate airflow and cooling requirements.
PCIe reset signal coordinated with power sequencing and reference clock behavior.
Present-detect and capability-encoding pins read by the baseboard to identify card presence and topology.
PCIe reference clock signals mapped with PERST# to host links across primary and secondary connectors.
PCIe wake sideband signal; v1.6 clarifies its relationship to Aux Power Mode and Main Power Mode.
